title: Risks to Human Capital
abstract: We build a model with inalienable human capital, in which investors finance individuals who can potentially become skilled. Although investment in skill is always optimal, it does not take place in some states of the world, due to moral hazard. In intermediate states of the world, individuals acquire skill; however outside investors and individuals inefficiently share risk. We show that this simple moral hazard problem, combined with risk aversion of individuals and outside investors, amplifies the equity premium, lowers the risk-free rate, and leads to disaster states that fall especially heavily on some agents but not on others. We show that the possibility of disaster states distorts risk prices and affects wealth inequality, even under calibrations in which they never occur in equilibrium.

author.Mehran Ebrahimian: Department of Finance, Stockholm School of Economics, 11383 Stockholm, Sweden
author.Jessica A. Wachter: Department of Finance, The Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19104; and Securities and Exchange Commission, Washington, District of Columbia 20549

article.1. Introduction: The modern financial system has developed over many centuries. Basic causes of financial frictions, such as the cost of trading, have lowered substantially. Other, less obvious costs, such as certain contracting frictions, also appear to be much lower. The incentives to apply human ingenuity to solve the problem of the flow of capital appear as strong as ever. Yet it is apparent that even now, not all individuals are able to raise capital for worthy projects, both in countries that are relatively wealthy and in those that are less so. In this paper, we study a very basic moral hazard friction in a setting with risk-averse individuals who have the potential to become much more productive following a risky human capital investment. We study the partial and general equilibrium implications of the fact that such individuals cannot perfectly commit to an optimal level of effort. We show that, in equilibrium, besides exacerbating wealth inequality, these frictions lead to higher aggregate risk for all investors, raising volatility and the equity premium. In the model, some agents have the potential to become skilled by making an investment in their human capital.1It is first best for all such agents to make such an investment; however, some of them do not have a sufficient initial endowment. For these individuals, outside funding is necessary, and future cash flows must be pledged to outside investors. At that point, however, a classic conflict arises: Individuals may obtain a greater benefit by failing to put forth effort if they have outside funding. Because of inalienable human capital, there are no means by which the outside investors may force them to do so. The result is that the project simply cannot be funded in certain states of the world. When many such projects cannot be funded, what otherwise would have been a mild recession becomes much deeper. Risk aversion of the potentially skilled individual is important because it affects the optimal contract: consumption of the skilled individual must not only be high, it must also be stable. Risk aversion of the outside investor is important because it affects state prices. It becomes expensive to compensate the individual in adverse states. Besides allowing the model to speak to puzzles in asset pricing such as the equity premium and the risk-free rate, incorporating risk aversion into a contracting framework can help explain procyclical dividends, “sticky” wages, countercyclical labor income risk and lack of stock market participation. One can think about risk aversion as worsening the coordination failure that lies at the heart of moral hazard models. Under adverse economic shocks, there occurs (to paraphraseBernanke et al. 1999) a divergence of interests between the skilled individual and the supplier of external funds. Unlike in classic asset pricing studies, this implies that credit will be rationed. It is not that funding becomes more expensive; it is that under some states of the world, worthy projects cannot advance at all. Our paper relates, first and foremost, to a classic literature on optimal contracting in the presence of moral hazard (Hart and Moore 1994,Aghion and Bolton 1997,Holmstrom and Tirole 1997). What our paper adds to this literature is risk aversion of both parties. The presence of risk aversion allows us to explore quantitative general equilibrium implications such as the equity premium, volatility, and wealth inequality. A more recent literature in macro-economics emphasizes the role of moral hazard and limited commitment in deepening recessions, based on investments in physical capital (Kiyotaki and Moore 1997,Bernanke et al. 1999,Kocherlakota 2000,Comin and Gertler 2006). These papers focus on the degree to which exogenous constraints amplify standard business cycle dynamics. Meanwhile, in the asset pricing literature one line of literature considers the implications of limited commitment for the equity premium, taking as a starting point the endowment economy ofLucas (1978), in which all agents can commit to make future payments (Alvarez and Jermann 2000,2001;Chien and Lustig 2010). These papers do not study production and hence do not have actions by agents motivated by moral hazard. More recently,Ai and Bhandari (2021)study asset pricing and labor market dynamics with imperfect risk sharing due to a two-sided lack of commitment in worker-firm relationships. Their paper focuses on the decision of a worker to remain employed in the firm, rather than to finance investment in human capital as cash-constrained individuals. They generate an equity premium through the desire of agents to hedge adverse changes in their consumption distribution through a preference for early resolution of uncertainty. In contrast, our intuition does not require dynamics and works through a deepening of shocks exacerbated by limited risk sharing. Our paper also relates to a literature on labor markets. First, we follow much of this literature in that we assume human capital is nonconvex; unlike an investment in physical capital, it is all-or-nothing and cannot be scaled up or down.Hansen (1985),Rogerson (1988), andChristiano and Eichenbaum (1992)argue that a nonconvex employed/unemployed distinction lies behind the volatility of unemployment relative to output, whereasPrescott (1992)argues that nonconvexities explain differential rates of unemployment among developed countries.Kehoe et al. (2019)derive nonconvexities from on-the-job learning and show that these contribute to unemployment volatility given variation in discount rates. This literature assumes that unemployment risk is perfectly shared and thus that markets are complete. Such an assumption diminishes the role of nonconvexities because individuals could replace their nonconvex opportunity with a convex one via a lottery. However, in our model, markets are endogenously incomplete due to moral hazard. It is not possible to enter into a lottery in which one pledges one’s future human capital. Although the previous literature focuses on explaining unemployment assuming complete markets, a second literature uses market incompleteness generated by unemployment to explain asset prices. Early work showed how countercyclical labor income risk together with incomplete markets could offer a solution to the equity premium puzzle (Constantinides and Duffie 1996,Storesletten et al. 2007). Countercyclicality in labor income can arise from the time-varying incentives of firms to hire and retain workers (Kilic and Wachter 2018); as mentioned previously,Ai and Bhandari (2021)show how this time variation can amplify negative productivity shocks, thereby explaining the equity premium in a production economy with limited commitment and a preference for early resolution of uncertainty. A second recent line of work focuses on the role of negative skewness (Constantinides and Ghosh 2017,Catherine 2022,Schmidt 2022).Guvenen et al. (2014)show that labor income of those on the low end of the income scale displays greater countercyclicality relative to the labor income of those at the top. Our model endogenously generates negative skewness through financial contracting frictions while also generating an increased equity premium as inConstantinides and Duffie (1996). Our paper also relates to a literature on the consequences of imperfect financial markets for economic development. In this literature, financing frictions hinder some individuals from making positive net present value investments in human capital. Individuals remain poor and continue to face the same financing problem; thus the economy stagnates into a “poverty trap” (Banerjee and Newman 1993,Ljungqvist 1993,Levine 2005). This literature typically abstracts from aggregate risk. Such risk is the focus ofAghion et al. (2010), who argue based on exogenous credit constraints that long-term projects cannot receive funding in bad times. They present evidence that is also consistent with our model: tighter credit leads to both higher volatility and lower average growth. The remainder of the paper is organized as follows.Section 2describes the economic environment: the agents, the contracting environment, and the definition of the market equilibrium. It also characterizes the equilibrium. We show that the model gives rise to four regions defined by the aggregate state. In the highest region, risk is efficiently shared, and outcomes (both aggregate and individual level) are as in the frictionless case. In the next region, individuals and outside investors share risk inefficiently. All individuals invest in human capital and aggregate outcomes are as in the frictionless case, although asset prices are not. In the next region, individuals are indifferent between investing or not. A fraction does invest, output is lower than otherwise, and risk is inefficiently shared. Finally, in the very lowest region, no individual (who cannot self-finance) will invest in human capital. In some respects, this region resembles a frictionless economy because incentive compatibility does not bind. Risk is efficiently shared, but the economy operates at too low of a level.Section 3gives quantitative results. This section also gives consequences for the wealth distribution and welfare: We show that large welfare consequences accrue in environments with aggregate risk under financing frictions.Section 4concludes.

Model: We present a three-period model in which individuals and investors face a financial contracting friction due to moral hazard and limited liability, similar toHolmstrom and Tirole (1997)andAghion and Bolton (1997). As inHart and Moore (1994), the individual cannot sell claims to human capital. To this standard framework, we add aggregate risk and risk averse agents. 2.1. Setup 2.1.1. Environment. There is one nonperishable investment/consumption good and there are three types of agents: (i) a unit mass ofoutside investorswith aggregate initial endowmentei; (ii) a massmhofhigh cash-on-hand individuals, henceforthh-individuals, with initial endowmenteh; and (iii) a massmloflow cash-on-hand individuals, henceforthl-individuals, with initial endowment𝑒𝑙≪𝑒ℎel≪eh.2There are three periods in the model. In period 0, agents write financial contracts and trade Arrow-Debreu securities. The state of the world—aggregate total factor productivity (TFP)—is realized in period 1. Afterward an individual chooses her occupation and (potentially) calls for funds from outside investors based on the financial contract in period 0 to pay for the investment costs. Financial Contracting. Table 1summarizes the timing in the model. We assume that, att= 0, investors and individuals can write all forms of long-term contingent contracts, subject to (IC). We assume investors trade contingent claims in a competitive financial market. Namely, for investors, the returns to investing in human capital are equal to the returns to investing in the contingent claim market. We assume incentive compatibility constraints are enforced by paying negligible management fees. Implicitly, we consider a financial intermediary who sign contracts with individuals and set incentive compatibility constraints on behalf of investors. High-cash individuals may self-finance projects and freely trade state-contingent claims directly with investors on the margin, given the state price densities, in a competitive financial market. The same state price densities under perfect competition are used to set the value of financial contracts with low-cash individuals. To be more specific, we assume that intermediaries channel funds from investors to an individual at tiny management fees—intermediation technology is highly advanced and intermediation costs are infinitesimal. Given the perfect competition among investors and intermediaries and the costless/frictionless intermediation, therefore, an intermediary, by raising funds from investors, signs an optimal contract with an individual such that it maximizes the utility of the individual. The intermediary sells the claim on the resulting contract payoffs at the competitive prices of the Arrow-Debreu securities to the investors in the financial market. Hence, investors are break even when considering delegating funds to intermediaries or trading Arrow-Debreu securities in the market. Relation to the Literature. Hart and Moore (1994)explicitly introduce the concept of inalienable human capital. They consider a contracting problem between an individual (debtor) and outside investor (creditor), both of whom are risk neutral. The debtor cannot commit to a contract that the two have agreed upon at an earlier time, and in fact, she will repudiate the contract if it is not currently in her interest. The reason for this repudiation threat is that the lender cannot force her to honor the contract: At no time can she be forced to accept a contract that is worth, at that very moment, less than zero. What is left of the contract is essentially her human capital, it is inalienable in that it cannot be seized by the investor and she can remove it at any moment if she chooses to do so. In our model,δH, or rather the present value of this is the value of the human capital, and it too belongs to the individual. Despite having entered into a contract, the individual can refuse to deliver this human capital by shirking and capturing the private benefits. The only way that the outside investor can receive her share is to promise the individual at least what she would get by shirking. As inHart and Moore (1994), the human capitalδHcannot be seized. Hart and Moore (1994)assume all agents are risk neutral and that there are no shocks to the economy. Linearity of preferences implies that one reason for contracts: namely risk-sharing and borrowing against future income, is not a focus of their framework. It is, however, a focus of an extensive literature on limited commitment in exchange economies (Kehoe and Levine 1993,Alvarez and Jermann 2000,Kehoe and Perri 2002,Kocherlakota and Pistaferri 2009,Chien and Lustig 2010). As mentioned in the Introduction, our paper departs from this literature in that it includes a moral hazard problem with production. Should the efficient outcome not take place, output will be lost (this will not happen, however, in equilibrium). Moreover, should the optimal contract imply that the traditional sector is chosen (as could happen in equilibrium), again, less overall output is produced. In exchange economies, however, the income is earned even if contracts cannot be enforced. Risk may be inefficiently shared, and agents may be unable to smooth, but the total quantity of income remains the same. That is not the case in our setting.6 Conversely,Gertler and Kiyotaki (2010)consider a moral hazard problem that causes output to fall below what it would be in a frictionless economy. However, in contrast to our setting, risks are efficiently shared. Bankers, who are subject to the moral hazard constraint, return every evening to share risks with lenders, implying that asset prices are the same as they would be in a production economy with the specified cash flows. That is not the case in our setting. The need to enforce the incentive compatibility constraint will also limit risk sharing. Another fruitful setting in the literature is one in which fund managers or financial intermediaries invest funds on behalf of agents. These intermediaries, if they do not have sufficient equity, might divert funds prior to the realization of the aggregate state (He and Krishnamurthy 2012). Contracts that require skin-in-the-game can prevent this, but also lead to less inefficient investment if equity falls below a certain value (“balance-sheet recessions”). One question pertains to the types of contracts considered.Cvitanić and Xing (2018)note that conditioning on quadratic variation is important and solve for the optimal contract in this type of setting with constant absolute risk aversion utility.Di Tella (2017)derives the optimal contract withEpstein and Zin (1989)utility and shows that this contract can eliminate balance sheet recessions unless there is idiosyncratic risk. As will be clear below, the optimal contract in our setting only partially resembles an equity “skin-in-the-game” contract: For a large portion of the space, it is closer to debt. In our discrete-time setting, the tightness of financing constraint is determined by the realized productivity and is not fixed and known ex ante. Recessions are worsen, even under the optimal contract and even without idiosyncratic risk in production opportunities. In the original setting ofHart and Moore (1994), a balance sheet constraint emerges because the debtor can always negotiate the creditor down to liquidation value (the creditor cannot commit to a tough policy). Balance sheet constraints, which are also plausible from an empirical perspective, have been a common assumption in the literature, including the classic paper ofKiyotaki and Moore (1997)and more recent work such asBrunnermeier and Sannikov (2014),Elenev et al. (2018), and others. In our model, one can viewfas the collateral, as the investor can always elect a contract under which he or she receives at leastfin some states. This is an ex ante choice, however. In our benchmark model, we assume full commitment on the part of outside investors. That is, the investor will continue to fund the project, even if her payment is less thanf.7InAppendix C, we solve for the problem of no commitment on the part of outside investors.l-individuals would no longer be able to invest in human capital in some states of the world. Finally, it is useful to contrast the nature of the financing friction in our paper with many papers in macro-finance literature with collateral constraints under limited commitment. In standard models of investment in physical capital, entrepreneurs may post a fraction of the initial stock of capital as the collateral for the loan. Financing capacity would then be state-independent—backward-looking. Whether the project is financed or not depends solely on the asset collateralizability, not the expected cash flow of the project. In the case of investment in human capital, there is no collateral to be seized and sold on the market (given modern institutions). We assume individuals in the model can only pledge the resultant cash to investors. Financing frictions endogenously arises because of (i) inalienability of human capital and (ii) moral hazard problem: Individuals need to have enough incentives to put forth a productive effort needed for human capital formation and a larger output in the future period. Financing friction is then forward-looking and endogenously gets less severe in good economic times, as there would be more pledgeable output. Other studies also use and test forward-looking financing frictions in various contexts in the literature.Paulson et al. (2006)show that a forward-looking friction based on moral hazard is the key driver of business startups in rural and semiurban areas in Thailand.Brooks and Dovis (2020)show that the pattern of exports upon Colombia’s trade liberalization is explained by an endogenous debt limit implied by forward-looking financing frictions.Bai et al. (2018)explain capital misallocation across Chinese firms via endogenous borrowing constraints in a forward-looking setup.Ebrahimian and Firooz (2023)discuss the role of output prices in forward-looking financing frictions to explain cross-industry profits in financially underdeveloped countries with trade barriers. In our paper, we discuss the forward-looking nature of financing frictions in the context of investment in human capital, especially under aggregate risks in the economy. 2.2. Financing constraints bind only in some states of the world, and when they bind, they may either only distort risk sharing or both risk sharing and aggregate output. Conversely,Kiyotaki and Moore (1997)and many papers in the literature assume that financing constraints bind in the steady state and approximate business cycles via log-linearizing around the steady state, which by definition ignores nonlinearities in the tightness of financing constraints and equilibrium outcomes. Via a novel recursive approach,Ai et al. (2020)fully characterize the nonlinear equilibrium dynamics in an infinite-horizon model with limited commitment. The intermediaries’ balance sheets associate with different equilibrium regions, and the region with a tighter constraint features a disaster state in which output sharply falls. In their setup,higherproductivity is associated with a tighter financing constraint, as agents would like to scale up the operation, given a high Marginal Productivity of Capital. In contrast, in our setup production function is nonconvex and the production scale is fixed; financing constraints are more severe rather inless-productive states, as there is not much return to pledge to outside investors. Constrained Efficiency Is the market equilibrium outcome a constrained-efficient allocation? In this section, we argue that the outcome of investors’ portfolio choice and individuals’ contracting problems given state price densities, together with the market clearing condition specified in the previous section, is indeed a decentralized outcome of a constrained-efficient allocation program. In what follows, we describe the centralized allocation program with moral hazard constraints for low-cash individuals and the solution to this program. We further show that the solution to this program resembles the outcome of the market equilibrium, which will constitute a decentralization theorem. Last, we argue that decentralization via only restrictions on individuals’ portfolio choices in a market setup, as the traditional fashion in the literature, is indeed not applicable in our setup. 2.4.1. We provide further details inAppendix D. 2.4.2.1. Relation to the Literature. We emphasize that the decentralization of the constrained-efficient outcome requires long-term financial contracting with the low-cash individual in time 0, which determines both the optimal consumption (implied by the portfolio choices in time 0) and the optimal occupational choices (undertaken in time 1). Importantly, a set of constraints on portfolio choices in an otherwise standard market economy with Arrow-Debreu securities (as characterized inAlvarez and Jermann 2000) is not sufficient to achieve the constrained-efficient outcome in our setup. The key point in our setup is the endogeneity of limited commitment and moral hazard constraints for low-cash individuals with respect to investment choice. The incentive constraint binds in our setup only ifo= 1. Therefore, restrictions on portfolio choices need to be based on a commitment to the occupational choice set at time 0. A low-cash individual would meet a lower natural debt limit in time 0, given a planned occupational choice𝑜⁡(𝛿)=0o⁡(δ)=0for a particular state of the world, but may not commit to this plan in time 1. The individual deviates too= 1, whereas the deviated investment choice would have required a more restricted portfolio constraint in time 0 to implement the optimal effort in the second-best outcome. More specifically, the individual invests in human capital (o= 1) and then shirks to enjoy higher consumption𝛼⁢𝑓>𝑐⋆⁡(𝛿)=𝛽/𝜋⁡(𝛿)/𝜆𝑙α⁢f>c⋆⁡(δ)=β/π⁡(δ)/λl, in states of the world that she was not supposed to invest𝑜⁡(𝛿)=0o⁡(δ)=0under the constrained-efficient outcome. This deviation transfers higher utility to the individual, but hurts investors’ payoff in a suboptimal way. Regardless, we note that the market equilibrium outcome that we defined in the previous section, through long-term contracting with an intermediary that represents investors and determines both portfolio choices at time 0 and investment choice at time 1 for anl-individuals, with the calibration in the next section, is indeed a constraint-efficient allocation, whereas by definition, it is a decentralized outcome.

Occupational Choice The individual with high cash-on-hand chooses to invest in human capital in any state of the world. However, the low cash-on-hand individual’s occupation choice displays state dependence. For values ofδsufficiently low, it is suboptimal for the investor to pay the individual a sufficiently high wage to satisfy incentive compatibility (IC). Without incentive compatibility, the individual cannot commit to not shirk. This problem, endogenously, does not exist for the high cash-on-hand individuals, who invest using their own funds and receive both the dividend and the wage. Figure 1shows the occupation choice of the low cash-on-hand individual as a function of the state of the worldδ.24This choice is always one for sufficiently highδ. There is a region in which equilibrium determines the fraction of agents who invest and who do not. This region is small. Forδsufficiently small, occupational choice is zero. The case with a convex technology (which we solve for inAppendix B) also features state dependence but is a smooth function ofδ. 3.3. Consumption, Dividends, and Asset Prices Figure 2shows aggregate consumption as a function ofδ. In the frictionless case, all agents consume in proportion to TFP. In the case with frictions, aggregate output (and hence consumption) is equal to that of the frictionless case, as long as individuals make the efficient occupational choice. In the lowest states, agents no longer make efficient occupational choices and aggregate consumption drops at the point in the distribution where agents stop investing in human capital. In the case of a convex occupational choice, aggregate consumption is lower, but the effect is smooth over the outcomes ofδ. Namely, disasters are more rare in the nonconvex case, but they are also more severe. The similarity between consumption in the frictionless case and in the case with frictions (especially in normal times) masks sharp distributional differences. These distributional differences turn out to be important for asset pricing.Figure 3(a)shows the dividend to investors: whereolis the fraction ofl-individuals who invest in human capital.Figure 3(b)shows the payoff to low cash-on-hand individuals. These two payoffs show how investors and low cash-on-hand individuals share aggregate risk. For most of the range of TFP, investors bear nearly all of the risk. Again, this effect is present for a convex opportunity set but is amplified in the nonconvex case. The consumption of low cash-on-hand investors is flat over this range, whereas, relative to the frictionless case, the dividend displays excess sensitivity to the aggregate state. Excess dividend sensitivity is an endogenous outcome of the contracting friction. The investor “overcompensates” the individual in certain states of the world because otherwise the individual would not put forth effort. The state-price density reflects imperfect risk sharing, asFigure 4shows. In the frictionless case, the state-price density is proportional to the inverse of the aggregate consumption, which in turn is proportional to the inverse of TFP. Bad states receive higher prices, as in the standardLucas (1978)economy. Compared with the case with no frictions, frictions imply a state-price density that is weakly higher at every level ofδ. At very high values ofδ, the state-price densities are the same (the economy is close to the frictionless case, because all individuals consume enough to make effort optimal). At the lowest values, they are also nearly the same (investment in human capital is not optimal in the world with frictions andbarelyefficient in the frictionless world). For most of the state space, state prices are quite different. The increase in the state-price density relative to the frictionless case reflects an intertemporal distortion. Consider the decision of the low cash-on-hand individuals. In the frictionless case, the value of human capital becomes a traded asset, and the individual’s consumption and savings decision is determined only by the standard Euler equation. In the case with frictions, however, human capital is not traded, and the individual cannot commit to provide effort unless he or she receives sufficient compensation.25This compensation amounts to a claim on the project. By internal-financing (consuming less today, in return for earnings from the project in the future), the agent shrinks the region in which efficient investment fails to take place. Internal financing the project is, of course, a form of savings, in that it shifts consumption from the time 0 to time 2. This savings raises the prices of all future states relative to the current state (because investors need to buy the claims to these states), lowering the equilibrium interest rate. There is a second channel leading to a lower interest rate. In equilibrium, outside investors must bear more risk relative to the frictionless case. Besides leading to a more volatile state-price density, it also leads to a greater precautionary motive, and thus higher state-price densities overall. It is also of interest to compare the state-price density under the convex and nonconvex technologies. One might think that the convex case would be a smoothed version of the nonconvex case. For many outcomes, this is correct: It is a smoothed version of occupational choice (Figure 1), aggregate consumption (Figure 2), and evenl-individual per-capita consumption in period 2 (Figure 3).26However, the state-price density under the convex case is qualitatively different than that under the nonconvex case. When the technology is not convex, the state-price density is nonmonotonic in TFP: There is a level ofδat which it spikes upward and then sharply falls. In contrast, the convex technology implies that the state-price density monotonically declines in the state. This upward spike is purely due to imperfect risk sharing. According toFigures 1–3, the convex and nonconvex case differ for values ofδroughly below the median value. Except in the left tail, in this range all individuals invest in human capital when opportunities are nonconvex. Under the case of convex technology, all individuals invest, but they do not invest the full amount. Although aggregate consumption is higher for some outcomes in the nonconvex case, risk sharing is less efficient, as seen in the sharply higher state-price density and in the lower dividend to outside investors.27In all cases, aggregate consumption is approximately the same (except in the lowest region). However, for individual outcomes, and for the equity premium, the form of the technology clearly matters. These figures highlight the differences between our economy and complete-market endowment and production economies and even economies with financial frictions such asGertler and Kiyotaki (2010). First, aggregate consumption exhibits a disaster state relative to TFP (Figure 2). Second, individuals of differing levels of endowments have quite different consumption profiles and exposure to the disaster. Specifically, the low cash-on-hand individuals are nonparticipants in the equity market, explaining the nonparticipation puzzle (Guvenen 2009) among individuals with low financial wealth—Campbell (2016)shows that these agents are much more likely to be nonparticipants.28Third, dividends are procyclical (in the many normal times), a fact that matches the data, but which endowment economies take as a given. Production economies, conversely, struggle to match this result (Kaltenbrunner and Lochstoer 2010). In a standard production setting with time-varying investment opportunities, dividends counterfactually fall in good economic states. In our model, however, dividends rise more than consumption in good economic states. Finally, relative toGertler and Kiyotaki (2010), markets are incomplete, leading to a more volatile state price density, relative to what would be implied by aggregate consumption. The dividend and consumption dynamics also speak to puzzles in macroeconomics. The flat consumption profile of the low cash-on-hand investor indicates a wage that is insensitive to the economic state, without the need to assume complicated wage-setting procedures.29The model speaks to why economic growth—Multiple realizations of a good economic state might lead to a declining labor share, a well-known empirical finding (Karabarbounis and Neiman 2013).Figure 3shows that, relative to TFP,l-individuals give up some consumption (wages) in high states to meet the pledgeability constraint (combined with investors’ participation constraint) in less good states of the world. Thus, when times are good, investors benefit more than proportionally (Figure 3). Figure 3also highlights an important tradeoff in matching the data that will be ubiquitous in models with a moral hazard friction.30The high and volatile state-price density occurs because of imperfect risk-sharing, a problem that grows worse as productivity falls. However, for very low values of TFPδ(the states agents attempt to avoid), it is the low cash-on-hand individuals, not the investors, who bear all the risk. In these states, investors cease supplying external capital. Although the consumption of low cash-on-hand individuals, and the aggregate consumption sharply falls, dividendsrise. Because consumption for investors is higher in these states, the state-price density jumps downward—a nonmonotonicity reflected inFigure 4. This effect will hurt the model’s ability to match the equity premium puzzle. That said, the existence of this state determines the equity premium in the model in the first place. 3.4. Cash Flow Volatility and Asset Prices Table 3reports quantitative implications for asset prices and quantities. The mechanisms that explain the equity premium in the existing literature (high risk aversion, exogenous disasters, long run risk) are absent here, so it is not surprising that in the frictionless case the equity premium is 0.1% with an unrealistically high risk-free rate of 7.5%. Frictions cause the risk-free rate to fall by a factor of three and the equity premium to rise by a factor of four, in the case of the nonconvex technology. What are the quantitative effects of nonconvexity? Nonconvexities in the occupation set is in the core of a vast literature in labor economics, in explaining variations in employment/unemployment over time and across countries (Hansen 1985,Rogerson 1988,Christiano and Eichenbaum 1992,Prescott 1992,Kehoe et al. 2019). Plus, although the general wisdom is that technology functions with respect to physical capital can more easily be scalable, indeed the literature emphasizes the role of nonconvexities in forms of fixed entry costs and nontrivial minimum scale of operation in explaining the impact of financing frictions, especially on the extensive margins (Rajan and Zingales 1998,Becker et al. 2013,Midrigan and Xu 2014,Ebrahimian and Firooz 2023). Nonconvexities with financing frictions and endogenous market incompleteness under aggregate risks in our setup brings in new quantitative insights for cash flow and output volatility, as well as asset prices. Indeed quantitative results for the state-price density, and the equity premium are much smaller in the case of the convex occupational choice, as compared with the nonconvex case. Nonconvexities amplify the effects of financial frictions on these variables by generating a dividend stream that is more than twice as volatile than the convex case. The payoff to low cash-on-hand individuals is more volatile as well. Consumption for thel-individual has a bimodal distribution in the nonconvex case but not in the convex case. Thel-individual is more likely to find herself in a very low state in this case than when occupational choices are convex. These states represent large shifts to the left relative to better economic times. In our model, consumption and wages are the same forl-individuals. The extreme sensitivity of wages at the low end to aggregate market conditions (Guvenen et al. 2014) suggests that nonconvexities are an important aspect of the investment in human capital with aggregate risks. Table 3also shows the effect of raising the return to human capitalH, and the effect of increasing the mass of low cash-on-hand individuals to one. IncreasingHand setting𝑚𝑙=1ml=1increases the equity premium to close to 1%, which is an order of magnitude larger than the frictionless value, even despite the assumption of log utility. 3.5. Comparative Statics The last section mentions that the mass of low cash-on-hand individuals and the return to human capital investment affect the equity premium. This section uses comparative statics to explore the mechanism underlying these results. We first consider the effect of raisingml. Comparing the left and right panels ofFigure 5shows that raisingmldo not cause a systematic change on the fraction of low cash-on-hand individuals choosing to invest in human capital. It is not obvious that there should be a change as this figure shows occupational choiceperindividual (it does not reflect the fact that the total number of individuals investing in human capital is smaller). Indeed there would be a wider region over which a fraction of individuals invest in human capital which makes the disaster states less severe and sharp (Figure 6). Changingmlaffects the per-share dividend (Figure 7), as well as per-capita consumption of thel-individual (Figure 8), in a second-order way. Mainly, it affects the state-price density (Figure 9). Figures 5–9show that a change inmlis on risk sharing (through the state-price density). The state-price density is both higher and more volatile (except in the very worst states) if more individuals require outside financing. Whenmlequals one, these individuals are the sole producers of the time-2 consumption good. Moderately low states, in which they consume too much, relative to their marginal product, are thus extremely costly. It may seem surprising that increasing the return to human capital increases the equity premium and the quantity of risk in the economy. To further investigate this effect, inFigure 10, we show solutions as a function of the excess return to human capital(𝐻−𝐻0−𝑓)/𝑓(H−H0−f)/f. The benchmark calibration (represented inTable 3) corresponds to(𝐻−𝐻0−𝑓)/𝑓=0.079(H−H0−f)/f=0.079. Figure 10(a)shows the disaster probability, where a disaster state is one in which individuals do not fully invest in human capital. The plot shows averageunder-investment in human capital. The higher theH, the lower is the disaster probability. This makes sense: A higher value ofHmakes it easier to persuade outside investors to participate, given the need to compensate individuals. It is therefore not surprising that expected aggregate consumption in the second period is also more close to the frictionless values asHincreases (Figure 10(b)).Figure 10(c)shows that aggregate consumption volatility is decreasing inHas well, as disaster probability falls. For the values ofHlarger than our benchmark calibration, the volatility lies in the range 3.5%–4%. In all cases, there is a value ofHsufficiently large for which all individuals fully invest in human capital across all states (so that the disaster probability equals zero), and for which aggregate consumption is equal in expectation to the frictionless case. ForHimplying a probability equal to zero, but close to the boundary at which the probability exceeds zero, second period aggregate consumption is as in the frictionless benchmark. In this economy, an econometrician would not see any disasters. This is an endogenous outcome; the disasters still matter, and agentsimperfectlyshare risk to (successfully) avoid them. Figure 10(d)shows the risk-free rate as a function of(𝐻−𝐻0−𝑓)/𝑓(H−H0−f)/f. The risk-free rate figure has a very different pattern than the disaster probability and aggregate consumption. For values ofHfar above those that generate observable disasters, and for which aggregate consumption appears identical to the frictionless case, distortions caused by the financial friction still matter. Agents still have a greater precautionary motive, and the interest rate is still depressed toward its zero lower bound for virtually the entire region we consider. This figure emphasizes the result that the disaster probability of zero inFigure 10(a)and well-behaved aggregate consumption are endogenous outcomes. Avoiding disasters has a strong effect on asset prices. Figure 10(e)shows the equity premium. The equity premium is nonmonotonic in(𝐻−𝐻0−𝑓)/𝑓(H−H0−f)/fin the nonconvex case, illustrating the tradeoff we described inSection 3.3. Lower values of(𝐻−𝐻0−𝑓)/𝑓(H−H0−f)/fclearly worsen the friction. They raise the probability that some individuals fail to invest (the disaster probability inFigure 10(a)), and they lower the risk-free rate inFigure 10(b). However, they lead to alowerequity premium because outside investors offload the risk onto the low cash-on-hand individuals. In low states of the world, investment is suboptimal, leading to higher values of dividends. To summarize, the lower is(𝐻−𝐻0−𝑓)/𝑓(H−H0−f)/f, the greater the friction, and thelessrisky the dividend. Thus, the equity premium increases in(𝐻−𝐻0−𝑓)/𝑓(H−H0−f)/f, even as the probability of disaster falls, and the risk-free rate rises. However, for sufficiently high values of(𝐻−𝐻0−𝑓)/𝑓(H−H0−f)/f, the friction becomes sufficiently unimportant, and risk comes closer to being perfectly shared. The equity premium still is above the frictionless benchmark, but it start to decline inH, because, over this range, higherHimplies that there is sufficient funds to meet the incentive compatibility constraint without too much distortion in risk sharing. Finally,Figure 10(f)depicts a substantial distortion on return volatility compared with the frictionless case, which reflects the volatility of dividend claims and consumption ofl-individuals. Numbers range around 10% (similar to the report in our benchmark calibration) but tend to fall (rise) asHincreases (decreases). As is the case for the risk-free rate and equity premium, the return volatility stays high, even if the observed disaster probability is zero and aggregate consumption is close to frictionless value. 3.6. Wealth Distribution and Welfare Our model has surprising implications for the wealth distribution and for the welfare of agents. Because of the log utility assumption, the marginal utility of wealth for the investor is simply equal to the inverse of the initial endowment and does not vary across economies. This is not the case for individuals endowed with human capital. For thel-individual, marginal utility of wealth (λl) is sharply higher in the nonconvex case compared with the convex case and is sharply higher in the convex case compared with the frictionless case. Because marginal utility of wealth is proportional to marginal utility of time-0 consumption at the optimum, this indicates that the individual cuts her time-0 consumption to rely on internal funds to cover investment cost as much as possible.32Interestingly, the marginal utility of theh-individual’s wealth islowerin the economies with friction. Thus, the gap in the marginal utility of wealth between high- and low-cash individuals widens due to financing frictions, which would further support a redistribution of cash from high to low cash-on-hand individuals from a utilitarian perspective. Panel B ofTable 4reports total wealth (in units of time-0 consumption), which includes both cash endowment and the net present value of human capital. For investors, this does not vary across economies, as the value of total wealth is simply cash-on-hand (the time-0 endowment). For bothl- andh-individuals, however, wealth is higher under financing frictions. This implies thataggregatewealth is higher in economies with frictions. This result seems surprising. Why is aggregate wealth higher, when, asTable 3reports, the equity premium attached to wealth rises, and aggregate cash flows fall? Moreover, why is aggregate wealth higher, even for thel-individual when the marginal utility of wealth is also higher? The reason is that the value of wealth masks the state-dependence of the payoffs at time 2, which is what matters for utility. This wealth is not something that a cash-poor agent is capable of selling, and turning into consumption in any manner that he or she pleases. For thel-individual, moral hazard constraints dictate that wealth must be consumed in a largely suboptimal state-independent way, except in the worst economic states, in which case consumption falls sharply. Intertemporal consumption choices are distorted as well:l-individuals consume more in period 2 in a suboptimal way. Moreover, moral hazard leads to a higher reservation payoff forl-individuals (as long as they invest in human capital, which is the case in 95% of the time in our benchmark calibration), which leads to higher state prices. The fact that state prices are higher overall hurts investors, whose wealth has not risen with the state prices to the same degree that the individuals’ wealth has. One can understand this result in terms of aCampbell and Shiller (1988)decomposition: Higher state prices overall imply a lower risk-free rate: the lower risk-free rate overwhelms the equity premium and cash flow effects. Theh-individuals are unconstrained, and in their case, wealth is proportional to the inverse of the marginal utility of wealth. The fact that their wealth is higher, and the marginal utility is lower, in cases with frictions, indicates that they are able to sell payoffs of investment in human capital at a higher value because state prices are higher. Conversely, consuming is more expensive. As Panel D ofTable 4reports their welfare (certainty equivalent value) considerably increases, but not as sharply as their wealth. Interestingly, the welfare of thel-individual in the end increases as well because she must be compensated at a greater reservation wage in the presence of moral hazard. The financial interests of bothl- andh-individuals are, to some extent, opposed to those of investors. Individuals are not hurt by the presence of financial frictions, and in fact, their wealth is higher (especially that ofh-individuals). By comparison, investors suffer a substantial utility loss due to financial frictions. The net present value of human capital is zero for all agents in the frictionless economy as individuals may freely invest in human capital, which competes away all the associated economic profits.33This is not the case under financial frictions (Panel C ofTable 4). Figure 11considers comparative statics for welfare. We show welfare (in consumption-equivalent terms) as a function of(𝐻−𝐻0−𝑓)/𝑓(H−H0−f)/f. In the frictionless economy, welfare for all individuals increases linearly in the quantity of goods (solid black line). For the high cash-on-hand individual, welfare is always more than the value in the frictionless case. This is the case, even for large enough values ofHfor which the observed disaster probability is zero. In this case, individuals invest in human capital anyway, and because of a lower risk-free rate they get a higher wealth and welfare. Relative to the frictionless economy, welfare of thel-individual increases in the benchmark calibration and in larger values ofHas they receive a greater reservation wage, but not so ifHfalls. Because of the failure to invest in human capital at higher odds in lower ranges ofH, thel-individual suffers a welfare loss relative to the frictionless case. The magnitude of this loss increases sharply with the disaster probability. Investors, on the other hand, suffer a substantial welfare loss that is positive at any point. For these investors, decreased risk sharing and over-compensatingl-individuals make them worse off. Unlike the individuals, they have no human capital to sell at a higher price to compensate. Importantly, aggregate risk is not, in and of itself, consequential for welfare. What matters is the interaction between the aggregate risk and financing frictions.Table 5reports welfare with predetermined TFP (𝜎⁡(log𝛿)=0σ⁡(logδ)=0) and compares this with our benchmark case. In a model without frictions, welfare loss due to uncertain productivity is negligible (compare the third and fourth columns, Panel D). This is a standard result regarding the welfare cost of business cycles in a frictionless economy with complete markets (Lucas 2003). However, uncertainty in TFP considerably lowers the welfare of al-individual in a model with frictions (compare the first and second columns, Panel D). Put it differently, the welfare impact of financial frictions crucially depends on riskiness of the economy. In a riskless economy the inalienability of human capital and the resultant financing frictions lead to ahigherwelfare for financially constrained individuals (compare the second and the fourth column, Panel D). Frictions lead thel-individual to require a high reservation wage; without uncertainty, this reservation wage is guaranteed. There is a pure redistribution of consumption goods from investors to individuals in the case with frictions as compared with the case without, assuming no risk. The overall impact of frictions and risk on welfare of financially constrained individuals is positive but not so if the average TFP falls and disaster probability rises (Figure 11). Outside investors suffer in any case as a result of financing frictions and aggregate risk. article.4. Conclusion: We introduced a model of inalienable human capital that we show can explain a number of macroeconomic and asset pricing facts. First, inalienable human capital exacerbates downturns, as it is no longer possible to compensate individuals for putting forth unobservable effort. This leads to a thick left tail in consumption, even if productivity is normally distributed. It leads to countercyclical labor income risk, as some agents no longer can finance human capital investments in low economic states. It also leads to dividends that are more volatile than consumption, and wages that are fixed. All of these are consistent with the data. Most importantly, financing frictions imply a lower risk-free rate, and a larger equity premium that is between four and eight times what it would be in the frictionless case. Although not explored here, our model is also consistent with a dynamic role of finance in asset prices. We have kept various elements of the economic environment fixed, such as financing frictions and the ability of outside investors to make commitments. Comparative statics in our model show that an increase in financial frictions leads to a greater equity premium, lower cash flows, and a “flight to quality”—a much lower risk-free rate. A change in the ability to form commitments also has similar effects. A generalized model has the potential to explain financial market fluctuations in terms of changes in the institutional environment. Studying the role of public liquidity might be another line of extension in our work. Individuals may better pledge the payoff of investment in human capital to the federal government compared with private investors, given the institutional advantages of the government. An example is wage garnishment Treasury offset in case of defaulting on federal student loans. Analyzing the role of public liquidity is interesting from both a positive and a normative view. We abstract from financial intermediation in our setup, by assuming that the incentive compatibility constraints in the optimal financial contracts with cash-constrained individuals are set by negligible management fees. A finite intermediation cost would have further implications for both asset prices and real outcomes and would be welfare-consequential both for individuals and investors. The interaction between intermediation costs, especially those that covary with the state of the world—namely, the balance-sheet channel, and fundamental institutional frictions explored in our setup: inalienability of human capital and moral hazard with limited liability, might be a topic for a future research project.

AItitle: 人力资本道德风险新解：内生股权溢价激增机制破题
AIsummary: **领域与核心问题**  该论文聚焦于金融经济学与宏观经济学的交叉领域，探讨不可剥夺的人力资本与道德风险如何影响资产定价、经济波动及财富不平等。传统研究假设完全市场或仅关注物理资本融资摩擦，难以解释现实中的高股权溢价、低无风险利率及劳动力收入风险逆周期性等问题。  **创新方法与贡献**  作者构建了一个包含风险厌恶个体与投资者的三期模型，引入人力资本投资的道德风险与有限承诺约束。研究发现，当外部投资者无法强制个体投入努力时，融资摩擦导致两类结果：一是部分经济衰退被放大为“灾难性状态”（低技能投资、低产出）；二是风险分担失效，推高股权溢价（较无摩擦基准高4-8倍），压低无风险利率，并加剧财富分化。模型通过校准显示，即使灾难状态均衡中极少发生，其潜在风险仍显著扭曲资产价格。  **突破与启示**  不同于前人仅关注外生信贷约束或完全市场假设，该研究将内生融资摩擦与一般均衡结合，解释了劳动力市场非凸性（如失业波动）与资产定价谜题的协同机制。其核心洞见在于：道德风险与风险厌恶的交互作用会内生放大经济冲击，形成“协调失灵”，从而为金融摩擦如何塑造宏观经济与金融市场提供了新的微观基础。
Highlights:
学术要点 1：不可剥夺的人力资本（Inalienable Human Capital） 指个体无法通过契约完全抵押的人力资本属性，导致外部投资者无法强制实施最优努力水平，形成道德风险的核心约束。|||
学术要点 2：内生性灾难状态（Endogenous Disaster States） 由融资摩擦在低生产率状态下触发的经济崩溃区域，表现为人力资本投资骤降和消费分布的厚尾特征，而非外生冲击导致。|||
学术要点 3：状态价格密度扭曲（State-Price Density Distortion） 因风险分担低效导致Arrow证券定价偏离经典模型，中间经济状态的价格密度显著高于无摩擦基准，形成资产定价异象。|||
学术要点 4：股权溢价放大效应（Equity Premium Amplification） 契约摩擦通过双重风险厌恶机制（个体与投资者）将标准股权溢价扩大4-8倍，同时压低无风险利率至历史低位。|||
学术要点 5：风险分担效率低下（Inefficient Risk Sharing） 在中间经济状态下，尽管所有项目被执行，但激励相容约束导致风险分担偏离帕累托最优，形成"协调失败"型市场不完全性。
